data.title: Corcept: Remains A Strong Buy On Recent Weakness And Aggressive Sales Force Hiring
data.description:
Corcept Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CORT) remains a strong buy as the recent weakness was created by huge put options trading, aggressively expanding its sales force, maintained 2025 and long-term revenue guidance and will be submitting a New Drug Application by September for an entirely different disease than what the company is known for.

Corcept’s fundamental medications help lessen the impact of the hormone Cortisol, known as the stress hormone, and is one of about 50 hormones in a human body. From the work the company has done to identify over 1,000 compounds it appears that mitigating cortisol can have positive effects on a wide range of serious diseases.

Weakness last week created by huge put options trading

On Monday last week, June 2, Corcept’s stock fell $5.88 from $77.56 to $71.68 with 6.1 million shares traded when about 1.7 million typically are. It dropped $2.17 or 3.0% on Tuesday to $69.51. Last week saw over 16,000 put options created or the equivalent of 1.6 million shares. The market makers who had to create them probably sold some number of shares to limit their exposure, creating downside pressure on the stock. This could have created a lot of downside stress on the shares.

While the shares have recovered 20% of the decrease over the past three days, they are still down 9.0% or $6.32 since Friday, May 30.

March quarter results fell way short of expectations

While Corcept generated $157 million of revenue in the March quarter, it only increased 7% year-over-year and missed expectations by $20 million. The company attributed the miss to problems at its single-source pharmacy (it has limited itself to one so that very tight prescription control is maintained due to the side effect of terminating pregnancies). The March quarter results in many ways mirrored the December quarter revenue shortfall as I previously wrote. While the company made its revenue guidance of $675 million for the full year, revenue was flat quarter-over-quarter versus the $11 million plus results the previous four quarters.

However, management stated the problem has been resolved with record tablets dispensed in March and April. Sean Maduck, Corcept’s President Endocrinology, said on the conference call “Our first quarter results were also affected by a greater portion of our business transitioning from branded Korlym to our authorized generic, which has a lower net price. As a result, our average price per tablet decreased by 13% relative to the prior year. We expect that the transition to authorized generic tablets will continue to grow this year, but that this decrement in price will be overwhelmed by an increase in the number of tablets shipped.” This is the first indication that Teva’s generic version has created price pressure on Corcept. However, over half of Corcept’s tablet sales are at the lower price so it doesn’t appear that the switch to generic versions will have a large impact.

EPS was a reported $0.17 vs. expectations of $0.14. However, there was a tax benefit of $10.9 million that added $0.10 to the bottom line. Therefore, adjusted EPS of $0.07 missed, which would be expected when revenue came up so short. Every $10 million in revenue would have added about $0.07 in EPS.

One positive on the income statement was research and development expenses falling almost $10 million from the December quarter. This verifies that the spike of $11 million in the December quarter was due to an inventory buildup of Relacorilant as the cost had to be expensed since it is not an approved medication, yet.

Management said single source pharmacy was overwhelmed but the problem is fixed

On the conference call Maduck said, “Our Q1 financial results do not reflect the tremendous patient growth our business is experiencing or what we expect for the rest of this year. Our first quarter results, specifically in January and February, were affected by insufficient capacity at our pharmacy vendor. As I said on our last call, the rapid growth in our business in the second half of 2024 overwhelmed the pharmacy's operational capabilities, and these challenges persisted into this year. That said, we have seen a substantial improvement in pharmacy operations in March and April. This has translated to a record number of tablets dispensed in March and April.” In a recent sell-side report it confirmed that the pharmacy issue was resolved in March and business has returned to normal.

In response to a question about meeting the potential higher demand for Relacorilant Maduck said, “there is the potential for a broader distribution network for that product, and that's something that we're actively exploring now.” Management got burned by not planning for a single point of failure problem and I suspect it will have at least one more, if not a handful of, specialized pharmacies that can supply Relacorilant.

2025 and long-term revenue guidance maintained

Corcept reiterated its 2025 revenue guidance of $900 to $950 million or growth of 33% to 41%, respectively, year-over-year. However, due to only increasing 7% in the first quarter the growth rates from April to December will need to be 41% to 50% year-over-year.

On the conference call Maduck, said “we expect the growth to continue through the second quarter and the rest of the year and actually accelerate in the half of the second half of the year.” Joe Belanoff, Corcept’s CEO, added, “we're not expecting an instant bolus. We just think the growth will accelerate through the entire year and then into the next year.” While these are very high growth projections, based on comments about the number of new patients, increased testing and aggressive salesforce hiring, they could be achieved.

This was the second quarter that management gave the same long-term revenue guidance of $3 to $5 billion in 3 to 5 years from the $900 to $950 million in 2025. This translates to 28% revenue growth of $3 billion in 2030 to 65% compounded growth for $5 billion in 2028. These revenue outlooks are only based on sales for hypercortisolism and does not include any benefit from additional medications.

June quarter results will be critical

While every quarter is the most important one before it is reported as a tech CFO once told me, I think the upcoming June quarter does fall in that category as there are very steep quarter-to-quarter revenue increases needed to get to $900 million in revenue this year.

The sell-side analysts estimates are:

March quarter: $157 million actual result

June quarter: $199 million, up $42 million from March or 21% growth year-over-year

Sept. quarter: $248 million, up $49 million from June or 36% year-over-year

Dec. quarter: $295 million, up $47 million from September or 62% year-over-year

Total: $899 million or 33% year-over-year

3 Reasons Corcept can make its aggressive revenue guidance

While analysts and outside observers can make projections, management of any company has much more information about its business and outlook. That being said, the combination of these three reasons are how Corcept can make its revenue guidance.

#1 Increased testing for hypercortisolism

Corcept’s findings from its CATALYST trial showed that 23.8% of hard-to-treat diabetics (those with an A1c level of 7.5 or higher) have hypercortisolism and that by taking Korlym it lowered A1c levels by 1.47%. These results became known last year which led to increased testing and subsequent very large growth in prescriptions for Korlym. It led to my analysis that there could be over 1 million people with hard-to-treat diabetes that Corcept’s medications could help.

With additional results to be presented at the American Diabetes Associations Scientific Sessions in two weeks and the publication of them in a few months, this should provide another boost to awareness and testing. On the conference call Maduck said, “our first quarter enrollments (meaning prescriptions) were almost double what they were in the first quarter of last year. And we've seen that continue on (conference call was on May 5).”

#2 Corcept is hiring salespeople aggressively

On the conference call Maduck said, “We currently have 125 clinical specialists, up from 60 at the beginning of 2024, and our plan is to have 175 in place before year-end in preparation for Relacorilant’s launch.”

I have been following Corcept’s job openings since February 28 this year. Over the past 14 weeks the company has hired 45 Endocrinology salespeople with 40 open positions still on its website, 10 Regional field sales managers for Endocrinology and in early May it listed 49 Oncology Account Managers positions. Note that since the conference call Corcept now has approximately 145 Endocrinology salespeople and has been hiring about 3 per week since the end of February.

To have such an aggressive hiring plan, Management must feel very confident about the increased testing, driven by educating doctors, on the higher than previously thought hypercortisolism in hard-to-treat diabetics and the need to introduce Corcept to oncology doctors.

#3 Major backlog of Korlym prescriptions

Based on my analysis, Corcept’s pharmacy shipped about the same number of tablets in the March quarter as the September and December quarters last year. This takes the 13.3% price pressure in the March quarter into account that was disclosed on the conference call and 10-Q (used on a quarterly basis vs. yearly stated on the conference call). It would indicate that the pharmacy had flat tablet shipments for three quarters in a row because revenue was flat from the September to December quarters and fell 13.6% from the December to March quarters.

This leads to the thought that there must be a major backlog of prescriptions to be filled as the company has said a record number of prescriptions have been written in the past few quarters and it wasn’t until the month of March that had record shipments.

Additional reasons Corcept will be successful

Corcept announced positive results for its Phase 3 ROSELLA trial of Relacorilant in Patients with Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer in March. The company plans to submit a New Drug Application in the United States by September and a Marketing Authorization Application in Europe shortly thereafter.

A major reason this is so important is that Corcept now has a medication that treats cancer vs. the current focus on endocrinology and hypercortisolism. It demonstrates that the company could create a totally different revenue stream and while it will require other trials to be successful, with over 1,000 compounds it appears that the odds are in its favor.

Oncology is the second (Endocrinology is the first) major platform Corcept is having success. The other two that are being tested are Neurology and Liver Disease.

To dive deeper into Corcept’s business and outlook check out its Investor Presentation.

Assuming Relacorilant is approved by the end of the year, it should negate the threat from generics, additional doctors should be willing to prescribe it since it doesn’t terminate pregnancies as Korlym does, has fewer side effects than Korlym and should increase retention rates due to the fewer side effects. The company could also reach out to patients that stopped taking Korlym due to its side effects.

Corcept generates very strong cash flows and has no debt. It currently has $571 million in cash and investments and depending on how much it spends on share buybacks (it spent $43 million in the March quarter at an average price of $54.84), it could have $1 billion by the end of 2026, or 13% of its current market cap.

Major risks revolve around Relacorilant being approved and Teva being successful with its lawsuits or generic version

There are two major risks to the shares both revolving around Relacorilant, Korlym’s follow on drug, not being approved by the FDA as it is always an unknown when and if a new drug will be given the thumps up. At least in this instance Corcept seems to have built a very strong case for the medication. The New Drug Application, or NDA, is based on the positive results from four trials or studies, one of which had patients on Relacorilant for up to six years. The NDA was filed on December 30 last year and on March 3 this year the FDA accepted the application. Note that this is the normal process for the FDA to first take about two months to accept the application and Relacorilant has a target date of December 30 this year for it to be approved or not.

The second biggest risk is Teva Pharmaceutical’s litigation to bring a generic version of Corcept’s Korlym medication to market. Corcept first sued Teva in 2018 for patent infringement and the latest ruling in early January 2024 was ruled in Teva’s favor, which Corcept is appealing. Teva also sued Corcept in June last year accusing the company of monopolizing the market for mifepristone-based Korlym. There is an appellate court hearing scheduled for July 7 with a decision sometime in the fourth quarter.

Besides having Relacorilant approved by the FDA, since it looks to be more effective than Corcept’s current Korlym, the new medication could largely shutout Teva from a large portion of the Cushing Syndrome market.

It does appear that Teva’s generic is only having minor impact to Corcept. While Corcept has seen over half of its patients move to its own generic, the 13% price differential appears to being overwhelmed by increased prescriptions and Corcept continues to provide the same level of support, no matter which version the patient it is taking.

Corcept’s shares are in a positive trend

Corcept’s price chart has been in a very positive uptrend since mid-2024 as the news about its addressable patient base grew over 100 times from 10,000 patients to over 1 million. From a technical analysis perspective, as long as it remains above its 150-day moving average the shares should continue their climb.

Corcept’s stock was slightly overbought before the drop last week with an RSI or Relative Strength Index of about 60. The RSI fell on Monday to around 43 and has moved back to 47.4, or essentially a neutral level.

The stock falling below its 50-day moving average could create some resistance for it to rise above $72.45 in the short-term. On Tuesday it traded above the 50-day but closed below it.

Build a position to take advantage of Corcept’s multiple strengths

While Corcept’s shares have already increased over 40% year-to-date, I believe they can move higher over the next year and especially over a two-to-four-year timeframe. This is based on the potential patient population of a million or more for hard-to-treat diabetes, additional revenue streams from oncology, potential from neurology and liver disease medications and being acquired from one of the large pharmaceutical companies. Another game changer would be positive results from its MOMENTUM trial for hard-to-treat hypertension (high blood pressure) late this year or early 2026.

Plan for volatility around the Teva lawsuit hearing on July 7, second quarter results being announced in late July/early August, the NDA filing for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer sometime in the September quarter, late October/early November for third quarter results, potential results from its MOMENTUM trial and finally the FDA decision on Relacorilant, hopefully no later than the announced December 30, if not a bit earlier.
